column2: Back
An LLM that dynamically decides its own control flow to reach a goal — acting via tools, observing results, and looping until a stop condition. = LLM + loop + tools + memory + goal + stop condition.

column1: Agent vs. workflow vs. single LLM call?
column2: Single call = no control flow. Workflow = predefined code paths. Agent = the LLM directs its own process and tool use at runtime.
Model/reasoning core, tools/actions, memory, planning/orchestration, control loop/policy.
Perceive -> Reason -> Act -> Observe -> Check (done/error/budget) -> loop or stop.
Max steps, token/cost budget, wall-clock timeout, repeated-action detection, explicit terminate/finish tool.
Pure LLM -> tool router -> fixed workflow -> single ReAct agent -> multi-agent -> autonomous self-directed.

column1: When NOT to use an agent?
column2: When a prompt/workflow suffices; tight latency/cost; high-cost irreversible errors without guardrails; or you cannot evaluate/observe it.
The engineering around the model — loop, output parsing, tool definitions/execution, memory, guardrails — that turns an LLM into an agent.
Connecting the model to real external data/state (e.g., via RAG or tools) so outputs are factual rather than purely parametric.
No — it trades predictability, evaluability, and safety for capability. Scale autonomy to the cost/reversibility of mistakes.
Curating exactly the right instructions, memory, tools, and data into the window at the right time, within a token budget. Broader than prompt wording.
Use the lowest level of agency that solves the problem; add complexity only when metrics demand it.
Prompting the model to produce intermediate reasoning steps before answering. Helps multi-step problems but has no external feedback, so it can hallucinate a chain.
Sample N reasoning chains and majority-vote the answer — a cheap reliability boost at N times the token cost.

column1: What is ReAct and why beat CoT for agents?
column2: Reason+Act loop: thought -> action -> observation -> repeat. Beats CoT because each step is grounded in real observations, so less hallucination, error recovery, and an interpretable trace.
Agent gets a feedback signal, writes a verbal self-reflection into episodic memory, and uses it to do better next attempt — verbal RL within a session, no weight updates.
ReAct re-reasons every step (adaptive, costlier). Plan-and-Execute plans once then executes with optional replanning (efficient for long, decomposable tasks; less adaptive).
Explore a tree of reasoning branches, evaluate intermediate states, and search (BFS/DFS/beam) with backtracking. Powerful but expensive; usually overkill in production.
Reasoning Without Observation: plan all tool calls up front, then execute, then solve — fewer LLM calls than ReAct (token-efficient) but less adaptive.

column1: How does prompting change with reasoning models (o-series/R1)?
column2: Do less hand-holding: give the goal, constraints, and tools and let the model plan internally. Over-specified step-by-step CoT can hurt them.
One LLM generates, a second critiques against a rubric, generator revises — loop until approved or budget hit. Use when there is a clear eval signal and iteration helps.

column1: When is reflection NOT worth it?
column2: When there is no reliable success signal (you reinforce noise), when latency/cost is tight, or on simple tasks. Always cap rounds.
Short-term = the context window (recent turns, scratchpad). Long-term = external stores (vector/SQL/KV/graph) persisting across sessions.

column1: Episodic vs. semantic vs. procedural memory?
column2: Episodic = past experiences/trajectories. Semantic = facts/user profile/knowledge. Procedural = how-to skills, prompts, routines.

column1: How to manage an overflowing context window?
column2: Rolling summarization/compaction, sliding window, retrieve-don't-stuff, offload artifacts to external memory, split across sub-agents, token-budget eviction.
Ingest: chunk -> embed -> index. Query: embed query -> retrieve top-k -> rerank -> augment prompt -> generate grounded answer with citations.
Chunking, embeddings, hybrid (dense+sparse) retrieval, reranking, query rewriting/HyDE, top-k, metadata filters.
Agentic RAG makes retrieval a tool the agent decides whether/what/where to use and can iterate on. Naive RAG is a fixed one-shot retrieve-then-generate pipeline.
LLMs attend most to info at the start and end of a long context and underweight the middle. Put critical content at the edges.
Poisoning (bad info stored/reused), distraction (irrelevant overload), confusion (conflicting/duplicate), clash (new info contradicts existing).
Separately: retrieval (context precision/recall) and generation (faithfulness/groundedness, answer relevance). E.g., RAGAS.
Vector DB for semantic similarity; knowledge graph for entities/relationships and multi-hop reasoning (GraphRAG). Often combined.

column1: How does function calling work? Who executes?
column2: Model emits a structured tool_call (name + JSON args); YOUR code executes it and returns the result as a tool message; model continues. The model never runs code itself.
Clear name/description, narrow scope, typed schema (enums), useful returns, idempotency/safety, few non-overlapping tools, errors as actionable feedback, token-aware outputs.

column1: How to handle tool errors for recovery?
column2: Return actionable error messages, validate args, retries/backoff, idempotency keys, timeouts, circuit breakers, graceful degradation/HITL.

column1: What is MCP and what problem does it solve?
column2: Model Context Protocol — an open standard to connect tools/data/prompts to models (USB-C for AI), turning N×M integrations into N+M.
Tools (model-callable actions), resources (readable data/context), prompts (reusable templates).
Host (the AI app) contains client(s) with 1:1 connections to server(s) that expose capabilities. Transports: stdio (local), HTTP/SSE (remote).
Use when no API exists (GUIs/legacy/web). Screenshot -> action loop. Risks: slow, brittle, expensive (vision), big security exposure -> sandbox + HITL.
Agent emits code that calls tools (composing several per step) instead of single JSON calls. More expressive; must run in a sandbox.

column1: Why not dump big tool outputs into context?
column2: Token cost, attention distraction, and overflow. Instead summarize/paginate or return a handle/ID.

column1: Why are parallel tool calls useful?
column2: Independent reads/actions run concurrently, cutting latency for I/O-bound calls. Only works when calls are truly independent.

column1: Single agent vs. multi-agent — how to decide?
column2: By coupling and context pressure. Single for tightly coupled work (avoid fragmentation). Multi for independent/parallel subtasks, isolated context, or specialization. Mind the token-cost multiplier.
A lead agent decomposes the task, delegates subtasks to specialist workers (often parallel), and synthesizes results. Most common production pattern.
Orchestrator-worker nested into a tree — supervisors of supervisors — for large, complex tasks.
Agents hand off many-to-many; most flexible but hardest to control. Usually constrained by a routing policy.
Control transfers between agents; one active at a time. Ideal for routing/customer support (triage -> specialist).
Agents share a conversation; a manager or round-robin picks the speaker. Debate improves reasoning/factuality at high token cost.
Agents read/write a shared workspace; a controller selects who acts. Good for opportunistic, loosely-coupled collaboration with a single source of truth.
Shared history, direct handoffs, shared state/blackboard, or structured artifacts. Biggest cost mistake: passing full transcripts everywhere — pass distilled artifacts.
MCP = agent-to-tools/data (vertical). A2A = agent-to-agent task delegation across vendors via Agent Cards over HTTP (horizontal).
Cascading errors, deadlock, context fragmentation, cost explosion, duplicate/conflicting work, lost-in-translation handoffs, hard failure localization.

column1: Why can multi-agent use ~15× more tokens?
column2: Repeated per-agent context, inter-agent chatter, parallel consumption, and orchestration calls. Justify with value; set per-agent budgets.
One agent produces, another reviews against a rubric, loop until approved — reliably improves quality (a.k.a. actor-critic / evaluator-optimizer).
Agent system as a stateful graph: nodes (functions/agents) + conditional edges over typed shared state. Supports cycles, persistence, HITL. Best for controllable production flows.
Conversable agents, often in a group chat with a manager selecting speakers; strong code execution. Best for collaborative, exploratory multi-agent tasks.
Role/goal/backstory agents + tasks + a process (sequential/hierarchical). Opinionated and readable; fast role-based teams.
Lightweight agents + handoffs + guardrails + sessions + tracing. Best for simple multi-agent routing on the OpenAI stack.
Microsoft enterprise SDK (C#/Java/Python): plugins/skills + planners + memory + agent/process frameworks. For enterprise governance and integration.
Data/RAG-first: best-in-class ingestion/indexing/retrieval plus agents and event-driven workflows. Use when knowledge/RAG is the core.
For a simple tool-calling agent — a ~50-line loop over the function-calling API is transparent and avoids lock-in. Add a framework for state/HITL/orchestration needs.

column1: How do MCP + A2A let you mix frameworks?
column2: They standardize the tool interface (MCP) and agent interface (A2A), so e.g. a CrewAI crew can call MCP tools and delegate to a LangGraph agent over A2A.
Align on requirements, Ground rules/risk, Establish approach (simplest), Nodes/components, Tools/memory/context, Safeguards/eval/scale — then trade-offs + MVP.

column1: First questions in an agent design interview?
column2: Goal/scope, users/volume, success metrics, latency/cost budget, error cost/reversibility, data/privacy, autonomy/HITL, integrations.
Router + handoff specialists, RAG KB with citations, per-user memory, confirmation+authz on destructive tools, HITL escalation, eval + tracing. MVP = FAQ RAG + human handoff.
Orchestrator-worker with parallel isolated researchers, a verify/citation agent, a writer synthesizer, budgets, factuality eval, and untrusted-web-content handling.

column1: Design pattern for a coding agent?
column2: Single-threaded ReAct + reflection on test failures, sandboxed tools, tests as success signal, optional reviewer on the diff, HITL on the PR, SWE-bench-style eval.

column1: Fix: a 3-minute agent task blocks the request?
column2: Make it async: queue + worker pool + status/streaming; checkpoint trajectories so long runs can resume.
Tiered routing: cheap model for routing/simple steps, frontier model for hard reasoning; caching; consider distillation for high-volume routine steps. Track $/task.
Non-LLM logic — validation, routing rules, math, schema enforcement — in code, not the model. Cheaper, faster, testable.

column1: How do you evaluate an agent?
column2: Outcome (task success) + trajectory (right tools/order, no loops). LLM-as-judge + programmatic checks + human calibration; offline regression set + online A/B; build evals from real failures.
Outcome = was the final result correct/good. Trajectory = was the process sound (right tools, order, no wasted steps/loops).
Pro: scalable, handles nuance. Cons: position, verbosity, and self-preference bias. Validate vs. humans, use rubrics and pairwise comparisons, randomize order.
tau-bench/tau2-bench, SWE-bench, WebArena, GAIA, AgentBench, BFCL (function calling), RAGAS (RAG).
Every LLM call (prompt/completion/tokens/cost/latency), every tool call (args/result/error/duration), the full path, and per-agent attribution. Tools: LangSmith/Langfuse/Phoenix + OpenTelemetry.

column1: How to stop an agent looping forever?
column2: Max steps, token/cost budget, timeout, repeated-action detection, surface tool errors into context, a terminate tool, then graceful degradation.

column1: Cut agent cost without hurting quality?
column2: Model routing, caching (prompt/semantic/tool), cap steps/tokens, compact context, trim tool outputs, parallelize, stream; track $/task.
Input (injection/PII/policy), output (schema/safety/grounding/action-authz), behavioral (budgets/limits/HITL). Enforce in code, not just the prompt.

column1: Where do humans go in the loop?
column2: Approve/reject before irreversible actions, edit plan/output, escalate on low confidence/repeated failure, and capture feedback for eval.
Version prompts/tools/models; run the offline eval set in CI and block regressions; canary/A-B; fast rollback; mine failures back into evals.

column1: Why are agents non-reproducible, and how to cope?
column2: Stochastic sampling + multi-step divergence. Lower temperature/seed where possible, deterministic code paths, full tracing, and evaluate over distributions not single runs.

Localize: check retrieval (precision/recall) -> fix chunking/hybrid/rerank; if retrieval is fine, fix generation -> answer-only-from-context prompt, faithfulness guardrail, citations, lower temperature.
Direct = malicious instructions typed by the user. Indirect = instructions hidden in retrieved/tool content (web page, email, doc) that hijack the agent — the signature agent threat.
Treat external content as untrusted; separate instructions from data; filter inputs; least-privilege tools/scoped creds; validate/HITL before destructive or exfil actions; break the lethal trifecta.
An agent with more permission/functionality/autonomy than it needs, so a failure can do real damage. Mitigate with least privilege, allow-lists, scoped creds, confirmation/HITL.
Private data + untrusted content + outbound communication. With all three, an injection can exfiltrate. Break at least one leg.
Trusting LLM output that flows into downstream systems (SQL/shell/code). Validate/sanitize, parameterize queries, never eval raw, sandbox generated code.

column1: Name key OWASP LLM risks for agents.
column2: Prompt injection, excessive agency, sensitive info disclosure, insecure output handling, supply chain (tools/MCP), data poisoning, unbounded consumption.

column1: How to roll out a risky agent safely?
column2: Shadow mode -> human-in-the-loop -> canary % -> full, with success metrics, guardrail thresholds, and instant rollback at each gate.
Higher = more exploration/variance (risk of bad tool args). Use low temperature for tool selection/argument generation; raise only where exploration helps.

column1: What do structured outputs / JSON mode give you?
column2: Constrained decoding guarantees schema-valid output, making tool arguments and parsing reliable and eliminating a class of parse-retry failures.

column1: Fine-tuning vs. RAG vs. prompting for domain knowledge?
column2: Prompting steers behavior fast. RAG injects fresh/private facts with citations. Fine-tuning bakes in behavior/style/format (not fresh facts). Often combined.

column1: What is distillation and why for agents?
column2: Train a smaller student model on a stronger teacher's outputs/traces to cut cost/latency for routine steps; pair with model routing.

column1: What are embeddings and why do agents need them?
column2: Dense vectors where semantic similarity = geometric closeness. Backbone of retrieval and episodic memory (RAG).

column1: Why do errors compound in agents?
column2: Each step conditions on prior state; success ~ p^n over n steps, so per-step errors multiply. Fix with short trajectories, checks, recovery/reflection, and grounding.
